---
title: >
  <table style="width:100%; border:none; border-collapse:collapse; background:transparent; cellpadding:0; cellspacing:0;">
  <tr>
    <td style="border:none; padding:0; vertical-align:middle; text-align:left;">
      <div style="font-size:1.25em; font-weight:bold; line-height:1.1;">Multilevel Modeling</div>
      <div style="font-size:1.25em; font-weight:bold; line-height:1.1;">in Clinical Research</div>
    </td>
    <td style="border:none; padding:0; vertical-align:middle; text-align:right;">
      <img src="`r here::here('slides','img','cfa_icon_emoji.png')`" style="width:150px; display:inline-block; margin-left:-10px;">
    </td>
  </tr> </table>
subtitle: "<small>Concepts and Applications</small>"
author: "`r paste(params$author, collapse = ' & ')`"
date: "`r paste0(params$location, ', ', params$date)`"
params:
  author: ["Author Name"]
  date: "Placeholder Date"
  location: "Placeholder Location"
---
exclude: true

```{r child = "_setup.Rmd"}
```

```{r title_emoji, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}

library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)
library(here)

# Create the graph with smaller arrows and transparent background
graph <- grViz("
digraph cfa_icon_emoji {

  graph [
    layout=dot,
    rankdir=TB,
    bgcolor=transparent,
    ranksep=0.25,
    nodesep=0.15
  ];

  // Nodes with thick black borders
  node [
    shape=circle,
    width=0.60,
    height=0.60,
    fixedsize=true,
    label='',
    style=filled,
    color='black',     // border color
    penwidth=2.5         // border thickness
  ];

  school [fillcolor='#FF8A80'];
  classroom1 [fillcolor='#FFB199'];
  classroom2 [fillcolor='#FFB199'];

  student1 [fillcolor='#FFD8C2'];
  student2 [fillcolor='#FFD8C2'];
  student3 [fillcolor='#FFD8C2'];
  student4 [fillcolor='#FFD8C2'];

  // Black arrows
  edge [
    arrowsize=0.6,
    penwidth=2,
    color='black',
    headclip=true,
    tailclip=true
  ];

  school -> classroom1;
  school -> classroom2;
  classroom1 -> student1;
  classroom1 -> student2;
  classroom2 -> student3;
  classroom2 -> student4;

  { rank=same; classroom1; classroom2; }
}
")


# Export to SVG
svg_graph <- export_svg(graph)

# Save PNG, transparent by default
rsvg_png(
  charToRaw(svg_graph),
  file = here::here("slides", "img", "cfa_icon_emoji.png"),
  width = 1200,
  height = 1200
)

```

---

#  About me

[Waylon Howard](https://wwwaylon.github.io/) (waylon.howard@seattlechildrens.org)

- Principal Biostatistician and Quantitative Methodologist at: [*Biostatistics, Epidemiology and Analytics in Research (BEAR)*](https://www.seattlechildrens.org/research/resources/bear) (SCRI) 

--
  - Research methods, statistics, and measurement
  - Grant writing, analysis planning, and manuscripts
  - Methodological innovation and applied solutions

<style="clear: both;">

.center[
.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
I help researchers answer the right questions!
</span>**
]
]

---

# Preliminaries

Slides and material are available at

.center[
https://github.com/wwwaylon/chbd-lgm-2025
]

- The talk consists of a combination of slides and hands-on exercises
- `r emo::ji('speech_balloon')` Feel free to ask questions anytime!
- We will primarily rely on [`R`](https://www.r-project.org/), [`RStudio`](https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/) & [`GitHub`](https://github.com/). 

**For my research, I typically use Mplus and R (`lmer`).**

---
  
.center[
## Software for Multilevel Modeling
]

.pull-left-45[
  
<small><small><small>
    
| Software | MLM Module |
|----------|------------|
| R | `lme4`, `nlme`, `xxM`, `DESeq2` |
| SAS | `PROC MIXED` |
| SPSS | `MIXED` |
| Stata | `xtmixed` / `mixed` |
| LISREL | `MULTILEV` |
| HLM | Purpose-built, widely cited |
| MLwiN | Strong for complex structures |
| EQS | Built-in |
| S-Plus | Built-in |
| SYSTAT | Built-in |
| Mplus | SEM + MLM, highly flexible |
    
</small></small></small>
    
]

.pull-right-45[
  
.center[
<small>

.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
My Recommendations
</span>**
]
   
</small>
]
  
<small>
    
**For research:**
  
<small><small>
    
> **Mplus** for flexibility with SEM and complex models    

> **R (`lme4`)** for reproducibility and custom extensions
  
</small>
    
No single tool is best for every situation - choose based on **model complexity**, **team expertise**, and **reproducibility needs**. Also consider factors like **collaboration**, and **cost**.
  
</small></small>
    
]

---
  
## Many names - same general idea

**Model frameworks**

<small>

> Multilevel models, Hierarchical linear models (HLM), Mixed-effects models, Linear mixed models (LMM), Growth curve models, Random coefficient models

</small>

**Model components**

<small>

> Variance components, Random effects / coefficients, Random intercepts and slopes, Varying coefficients, Intercepts- or slopes-as-outcomes, *Cross-level interactions*, *Contextual effects*

`r emo::ji('point_right')` **These are all variations of mixed models.**

</small>

---
  
## Plan Today
  
```{r schedule, echo = F, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Updated schedule (concise version)
schedule <- data.frame(
  "Time" = c(
    "0–10",
    "10–25",
    "25–40",
    "40–55",
    "55–85",
    "85–90"
  ),
  "Segment" = c(
    "Why is this important?",
    "Estimation in Statistical Models",
    "How not to handle nested data",
    "Empty means models & ICC",
    "Conditional models & Effect Size",
    "Next Steps & Extensions"
  ),
  "Key Concepts" = c(
    "Nested data in the real world",
    "OLS vs ML estimation",
    "Traditional methods & limitations",
    "Variance decomposition<br>Empty model & ICC",
    "Model building & effect size",
    "Extensions & resources"
  ),
  "Slides" = c(
    "1–6",
    "7–15",
    "16–25",
    "26–35",
    "36–52",
    "53–55"
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

knitr::kable(
  schedule,
  format = "html",
  align = "llll",
  escape = FALSE
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16,
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE, color = "#4B4F54") %>%
  column_spec(2, bold = TRUE, width = "23%") %>%
  column_spec(3, width = "47%") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#00A9E0", color = "white") %>%
  row_spec(2, bold = FALSE, background = "#00A9E0", color = "white") %>%
  row_spec(4, bold = FALSE, background = "#00A9E0", color = "white") %>%
  row_spec(6, bold = FALSE, background = "#00A9E0", color = "white")

# Seattle Children's official colors include:
#Teal ("#00B5AD")
#Bright Green ("#8DC63F")
#Light Blue ("#00A9E0")
#Warm Gray ("#A7A9AC")
#Dark Gray ("#4B4F54")
#orange ("#EB7B35")

```

---

## Why multilevel models? 
  
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.align="center", eval=T}

library(DiagrammeR)
library(DiagrammeRsvg)
library(rsvg)
library(here)

# Create the graph with larger circles but same figure height
graph <- grViz("
digraph higher_order_cfa {

  // Graph settings
  graph [layout=dot, rankdir=TB, fontsize=28, bgcolor=transparent, ranksep=0.6];
  
  // Make circle borders thicker
  node [shape=circle, penwidth=3];
  edge [penwidth=2];

  // Top-level hospital (largest circle)
  node [shape=circle, fontsize=32, width=2.5, height=2.5, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor='#F59A63'];
  school [label='Hospital 1'];

  // Clinics (medium circles)
  node [shape=circle, fontsize=28, width=1.8, height=1.8, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor='#F7B98F'];
  classroom1 [label='Clinic 1'];
  classroom2 [label='Clinic 2'];

  // Patients (small circles)
  node [shape=circle, fontsize=20, width=1.4, height=1.4, fixedsize=true, style=filled, fillcolor='#FAD9C6'];
  student1 [label='Patient 1'];
  student2 [label='Patient 2'];
  student3 [label='Patient 3'];
  student4 [label='Patient 4'];
  student5 [label='Patient 5'];
  student6 [label='Patient 6'];

  // Edges: Hospital -> Clinics
  school -> classroom1;
  school -> classroom2;

  // Edges: Clinics -> Patients
  classroom1 -> {student1 student2 student3};
  classroom2 -> {student4 student5 student6};

  // Align clinics horizontally
  { rank=same; classroom1; classroom2; }

  // Align patients horizontally per clinic
  { rank=same; student1; student2; student3; }
  { rank=same; student4; student5; student6; }

}")

# Export to SVG
svg_graph <- export_svg(graph)

# Save PNG, transparent by default
rsvg_png(
  charToRaw(svg_graph),
  file = here::here("slides", "img", "mlm.png"),
  width = 1200,
  height = 1200
)

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','mlm.png')` style="width:300px; display:block; margin:-60px auto -30px auto;">
]

<small><small>
  
- Data are clustered `r emo::ji('arrow_right')` observations are related  

- Standard analyses assume **independence**
  
  **Example: hospital re-admissions**
  
> *Patients in the same hospital share unmeasured factors.*  
> *Repeated measurements on the same patient are not independent.*
  
</small></small>
  
<style="clear: both;">
  
.center[
<small><small>
      
.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
Violations of this assumption bias standard errors. 
</span>**
]
</small></small>
  ]

---
  
## Kinds of Data Structure
  
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=4, fig.align="center", eval=T}
    
library(DiagrammeR)
    
graph <- grViz("
digraph horizontal_levels {

graph [rankdir=LR, nodesep=0.1, ranksep=0.1, splines=line, esep=0, bgcolor=transparent]
  node [shape=box, style=none, color=none, fontsize=16]

  // ---- Level 1 (left) with ports on each item ----
  level1 [
    label=<
      <TABLE BORDER='0' CELLBORDER='0' CELLSPACING='1'>
        <TR><TD><B><U>Level 1</U></B></TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='p1' ALIGN='RIGHT'>students  </TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='p2' ALIGN='RIGHT'>corporations  </TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='p3' ALIGN='RIGHT'>spouses  </TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='p4' ALIGN='RIGHT'>cells</TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='p5' ALIGN='RIGHT'>repeated measures    </TD></TR>
      </TABLE>
    >, 
  ]

  // ---- Nested within box (center) - tall, no internal ports ----
  nested [
    label=<
      <TABLE BORDER='0' CELLBORDER='0' CELLSPACING='0' CELLPADDING='1'>
        <TR><TD ALIGN='CENTER'><B>are nested    </B></TD></TR>
        <TR><TD ALIGN='CENTER'><B>within  </B></TD></TR>
      </TABLE>
    >,
  shape=box,
  style=filled,
  height=1.5,       // in inches
  width=1.5,
  fixedsize=true
  ]

  // ---- Level 2 (right) with ports on each item ----
  level2 [
    label=<
      <TABLE BORDER='0' CELLBORDER='0' CELLSPACING='1'>
        <TR><TD><B><U>Level 2</U></B></TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='q1' ALIGN='LEFT'>classrooms</TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='q2' ALIGN='LEFT'>nations</TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='q3' ALIGN='LEFT'>couples</TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='q4' ALIGN='LEFT'>biopsy samples</TD></TR>
        <TR><TD PORT='q5' ALIGN='LEFT'>individuals</TD></TR>
      </TABLE>
    >
  ]

  // ---- Converging lines: all Level 1 ports connect to west center of nested ----
  level1:p1 -> nested:w [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  level1:p2 -> nested:w [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  level1:p3 -> nested:w [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  level1:p4 -> nested:w [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  level1:p5 -> nested:w [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]

  // ---- Diverging lines: from east center of nested to all Level 2 ports ----
  nested:e -> level2:q1 [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  nested:e -> level2:q2 [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  nested:e -> level2:q3 [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  nested:e -> level2:q4 [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
  nested:e -> level2:q5 [color='#EB7B35', arrowhead=none]
}
")
 
# Export to SVG
svg_graph <- export_svg(graph)

# Save PNG, transparent by default
rsvg_png(
  charToRaw(svg_graph),
  file = here::here("slides", "img", "l1l2.png"),
  width = 1200,        # width in pixels
  height = NULL        # preserve SVG aspect ratio
)

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','l1l2.png')` style="width:600px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -40px auto;">
]   

.left[
  
<small><small>
    
<br>
    
- **Hierarchical (Nested)** repeated tests - students - schools - districts
  
- **Crossed (Non-nested)** observations within participants *and* tasks  
  
- **Balanced vs. Unbalanced** Experiments vs. Real-world    

- **Other Structures** Temporal (time-ordered), Spatial (location-based) 
  
]

Once you learn about hierarchical data, you start seeing examples everywhere!

<small><small>

*Image adapted from Preacher (2010) lecture - thanks Kris.*

</small></small></small></small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**How do statistical models estimate parameters?**

<small><small>

| Model | Outcome | Effects | Sampling |
|------|---------------------|--------|----------------|
| **Linear Model** | **Normal** | **Fixed** | **1 level** |
| <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Generalized Linear Model</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Any</span>  | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Fixed</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">1 level</span> |
| <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Linear *Mixed* Model</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Normal</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Fixed + Random</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">2+ levels</span> |
| <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Generalized Linear *Mixed* Model</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Any</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">Fixed + Random</span> | <span style="color:#A7A9AC;">2+ levels</span> |

</small>

**`r emo::ji("key")` Key question:**

> How do we estimate the parameters of these models?

We start with Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**

$$
\color{gray}{\text{Linear regression: }} \text{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{x}_i + \text{e}_i
$$

$$
\color{gray}{\text{Residual: }} \text{e}_i = \text{y}_i - \hat{\text{y}}_i
$$

$$
\small \color{gray}{\text{OLS chooses coefficients that minimize: }} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{y}_i - \hat{\text{y}}_i)^2
$$

**`r emo::ji("bulb")` Core idea**

> Find the line that minimizes the total squared prediction error.

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

```{r ols-squares-plot, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.width=9, fig.height=4.5, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

set.seed(42)
n <- 12
x <- seq(-5, 15, length.out = n)
y <- 0.9 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
df_ols <- data.frame(x = x, y = y)

fit <- lm(y ~ x, data = df_ols)
df_ols$yhat <- predict(fit)
df_ols$resid <- df_ols$y - df_ols$yhat

# Build square polygons for each residual
make_square <- function(x0, y0, yhat0) {
  side <- y0 - yhat0
  if (side >= 0) {
    data.frame(
      px = c(x0, x0 - abs(side), x0 - abs(side), x0),
      py = c(yhat0, yhat0, y0, y0)
    )
  } else {
    data.frame(
      px = c(x0, x0 + abs(side), x0 + abs(side), x0),
      py = c(yhat0, yhat0, y0, y0)
    )
  }
}

squares <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(n), function(i) {
  sq <- make_square(df_ols$x[i], df_ols$y[i], df_ols$yhat[i])
  sq$id <- i
  sq
}))

# OLS minimises the **total area** of the residual squares",
# Each orange dashed line is a residual; each blue square is its squared value"

p1 <- ggplot(df_ols, aes(x, y)) +
  
  coord_fixed() + #fix x-y aspect ratio else you get rectangles 
  
  # Data points
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.45, color = "#FF7F00") +

    scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$x)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$x)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")+ 
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$y)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05))
  )+
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title    = ggtext::element_markdown(face = "bold", colour = "#1E2761"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#4B4F54")
  ) 
  
ggsave(here::here('slides','img','ols1_plot.png'), plot = p1, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)



p2 <- ggplot(df_ols, aes(x, y)) +
  
  coord_fixed() + #fix x-y aspect ratio else you get rectangles 
  
  # Regression line
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#4B4F54", linewidth = 2.5) +
  
  # Data points
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.45, color = "#FF7F00") +
  
    scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$x)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$x)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$y)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05))
  )+
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)") +
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title    = ggtext::element_markdown(face = "bold", colour = "#1E2761"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#4B4F54")
  ) 
  

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','ols2_plot.png'), plot = p2, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)



p3 <- ggplot(df_ols, aes(x, y)) +
  
  coord_fixed() + #fix x-y aspect ratio else you get rectangles 
 
    # Residual segments
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x, yend = yhat),
               colour = "#00B5AD", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
   
  # Regression line
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#4B4F54", linewidth = 2.5) +
  # Data points
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.45, color = "#FF7F00") +
  
    scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$x)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$x)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$y)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05))
  )+
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title    = ggtext::element_markdown(face = "bold", colour = "#1E2761"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#4B4F54")
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','ols3_plot.png'), plot = p3, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)



p4 <- ggplot(df_ols, aes(x, y)) +
  
  coord_fixed() + #fix x-y aspect ratio else you get rectangles 
 
    geom_polygon(
    data = squares,
    aes(x = px, y = py, group = id),
     fill = "#8DC63F", colour = "#A7A9AC", alpha = 0.10, linewidth = 0.2
  ) +
  
  # Residual segments
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x, yend = yhat),
               colour = "#00B5AD", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
   
  # Regression line
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#4B4F54", linewidth = 2.5) +
  # Data points
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.45, color = "#FF7F00") +
  
    scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$x)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$x)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df_ols$y)),
      ceiling(max(df_ols$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05))
  )+
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title    = ggtext::element_markdown(face = "bold", colour = "#1E2761"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#4B4F54")
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','ols4_plot.png'), plot = p4, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)

# Seattle Children's official colors include:
#Teal ("#00B5AD")
#Bright Green ("#8DC63F")
#Light Blue ("#00A9E0")
#Warm Gray ("#A7A9AC")
#Dark Gray ("#4B4F54")
#orange ("#EB7B35")

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','ols1_plot.png')` style="width:400px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

<small>

This scatterplot shows the relationship between a predictor **X** and an outcome **Y**. Each orange point represents an observed score.

**OLS will search for the line that best predicts these values.**

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','ols2_plot.png')` style="width:400px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

<small>

**Features of the best-fitting line:** <small> Intercept $\beta_0$, Slope $\beta_1$, Residual variance </small>

These parameters define the model that best predicts the data.

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

.center[

<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','ols3_plot.png')` style="width:400px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">

]  

<small>

A **residual** is the vertical distance to the regression line: $\text{e}_i = \text{y}_i - \hat{\text{y}}_i$

But OLS does **not minimize the raw residuals**.

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**Why Square Residuals?**

<small><small>

| Strategy | Problem |
|----------|---------|
| Minimize **sum** of residuals | Positive & negative cancel |
| Minimize **sum of absolute** residuals | Difficult to solve |
| Minimize **sum of squared** residuals | Works `r emo::ji("trophy")` `r emo::ji("sparkles")` | 

</small></small>

<small>

Key benefits:

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` makes all values positive

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` penalizes large errors

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` produces a **closed-form solution**

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','ols4_plot.png')` style="width:400px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

$\color{grey}{\text{OLS chooses the line that minimizes: }}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{y}_i - \hat{\text{y}}_i)^2$

---

```{r, ols-animation, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=4.2, cache=TRUE, eval = F}

library(ggplot2)
library(animation)
library(here)
library(scales)
library(ggtext)

# ── Fixed data ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(1234)
n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 40

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 65)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))

# ── OLS solution ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fit    <- lm(y ~ x, data = df)
ols_b0 <- coef(fit)[1]
ols_b1 <- coef(fit)[2]

ssr <- function(b0, b1) sum((df$y - (b0 + b1 * df$x))^2)

x_range <- range(df$x)

# ── AXIS LIMITS: tight around data, then enforce 16:9 via coord_fixed ─────────
#
# The root cause of the whitespace problem:
#   coord_fixed(1) forces 1 data-unit-x == 1 data-unit-y in PIXELS.
#   Your X span (~100 units) >> Y span (~15 units), so to fill a 16:9 canvas
#   with equal-unit axes, Y had to be blown out to ~56 units — almost 4× the
#   actual data range, creating massive top/bottom whitespace.
#
# Fix: compute tight limits from the data, then set
#   coord_fixed(ratio = x_span / y_span * 9/16)
# This tells ggplot "one x-unit should appear (9/16)*(x_span/y_span) times
# as tall as one y-unit" — which makes the plot fill the 16:9 canvas exactly
# while keeping the squares visually square.

x_pad <- diff(range(df$x)) * 0.06
y_pad <- diff(range(df$y)) * 0.10   # slightly more vertical breathing room

x_limits <- range(df$x) + c(-x_pad, x_pad)
y_limits <- range(df$y) + c(-y_pad, y_pad)

x_span <- diff(x_limits)
y_span <- diff(y_limits)

# coord_fixed ratio that makes the physical canvas exactly 16:9
# and simultaneously renders residual squares as true squares
fixed_ratio <- (x_span / y_span) * (9 / 16)

# ── Keyframes ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
kf <- data.frame(
  b0 = c(ols_b0 - 6,  ols_b0 - 3,  ols_b0,
         ols_b0 + 3,  ols_b0 + 5,  ols_b0 + 3,
         ols_b0,      ols_b0 - 2,  ols_b0),
  b1 = c(ols_b1 + 0.08, ols_b1 + 0.04, ols_b1,
         ols_b1 - 0.03, ols_b1 - 0.07, ols_b1 - 0.03,
         ols_b1,        ols_b1 + 0.02, ols_b1)
)

# ── Interpolation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
n_interp <- 18

params <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(nrow(kf) - 1), function(k) {
  t_seq  <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n_interp + 1)[-(n_interp + 1)]
  t_ease <- (1 - cos(t_seq * pi)) / 2
  data.frame(
    b0 = kf$b0[k] + (kf$b0[k+1] - kf$b0[k]) * t_ease,
    b1 = kf$b1[k] + (kf$b1[k+1] - kf$b1[k]) * t_ease
  )
}))
params <- rbind(params, kf[nrow(kf), ])

# ── Subsample for squares ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(99)
sq_idx <- sample(nrow(df), 200)
df_sq  <- df[sq_idx, ]

make_squares <- function(b0, b1) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(nrow(df_sq)), function(j) {
    
    x0    <- df_sq$x[j]
    y0    <- df_sq$y[j]
    yhat0 <- b0 + b1 * x0
    
    side     <- y0 - yhat0
    side_abs <- abs(side)
    x_dir    <- if (side >= 0) -1 else 1
    
    # To appear visually square in a non-unit-ratio coord system,
    # the x-extent of the square must be scaled by fixed_ratio
    x_side <- side_abs * fixed_ratio
    
    px <- c(x0,
            x0 + x_dir * x_side,
            x0 + x_dir * x_side,
            x0,
            x0)
    
    py <- c(yhat0,
            yhat0,
            y0,
            y0,
            yhat0)
    
    data.frame(px = px, py = py, pt_id = j)
  }))
}

plot_frame <- function(b0, b1) {
  
  sq_df <- make_squares(b0, b1)
  
  df_line <- data.frame(
    x = x_limits,
    y = c(b0 + b1 * x_limits[1], b0 + b1 * x_limits[2])
  )
  
  df_local <- df
  df_local$yhat_temp <- b0 + b1 * df_local$x
  
  current_ssr <- ssr(b0, b1)
  
  label_text <- paste0(
    "<b><i>b</i><sub>0</sub> = ", round(b0, 2),
    "<br><i>b</i><sub>1</sub> = ", round(b1, 3),
    "<br><br>SSR = ", comma(round(current_ssr, 0)), "</b>"
  )
  
  label_x <- x_limits[2] - 0.03 * x_span
  label_y <- y_limits[2] - 0.04 * y_span
  
  ggplot(df_local, aes(x, y)) +
    
    geom_polygon(
      data = sq_df,
      aes(x = px, y = py, group = pt_id),
      fill      = "#8DC63F",
      colour    = "#A7A9AC",
      alpha     = 0.10,
      linewidth = 0.2
    ) +
    
    geom_segment(
      aes(xend = x, yend = yhat_temp),
      colour    = "#00B5AD",
      linewidth = 0.7,
      linetype  = "dashed"
    ) +
    
    geom_line(
      data = df_line,
      aes(x = x, y = y),
      colour    = "#4B4F54",
      linewidth = 2.2
    ) +
    
    geom_point(size = 6, alpha = 0.30, color = "#FF7F00") +
    
    annotate(
      "richtext",
      x     = label_x,
      y     = label_y,
      label = label_text,
      hjust = 1,
      vjust = 1,
      size  = 7,
      fill        = scales::alpha("white", 0.75),
      label.color = NA,
      label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines"),
      label.r       = unit(0.15, "lines")
    ) +
    
    scale_x_continuous(limits = x_limits, expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = y_limits, expand = c(0, 0)) +
    
    # Key fix: ratio = x_span/y_span * 9/16 fills the 16:9 canvas tightly
    # while making the residual squares appear visually square
    coord_fixed(ratio = fixed_ratio) +
    
    theme_classic(base_size = 38) +
    theme(
      axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 38),
      axis.text       = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
      plot.margin     = margin(t = 8, r = 10, b = 6, l = 6, unit = "pt"),
      legend.position = "none"
    ) +
    labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")
}

# ── Save GIF ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
saveGIF({
  
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(params))) {
    print(plot_frame(params$b0[i], params$b1[i]))
  }
  
  for (j in 1:20) {
    print(plot_frame(ols_b0, ols_b1))
  }
  
},
movie.name = here("slides", "img", "ols_squares_zoomed.gif"),
interval   = 0.1,
ani.width  = 1280,
ani.height = 720
)

```

.center[

`r knitr::include_graphics(here::here("slides", "img", "ols_squares_zoomed.gif"))`

]  

<small>

Geometrically:

> OLS minimizes the **total area of the residual squares**.

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

```{r, ols-squared-residuals, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=4.2, cache=TRUE, eval = F}

# Load libraries
library(ggplot2)

# Example: single data point
Y_i <- 5
X_i <- 2

# Try different beta1 values (beta0 = 0 for simplicity)
beta1 <- seq(-2, 4, length.out = 500)

# Residuals
residuals <- Y_i - (0 + beta1 * X_i)

# Squared residuals
squared_residuals <- residuals^2

# Put into a data frame
df <- data.frame(beta1 = beta1, squared_residuals = squared_residuals)

# APA-style plot
p6 <- ggplot(df, aes(x = beta1, y = squared_residuals)) +
  geom_line(color = "#00B5AD", size = 1) +              # black line is more APA-friendly
  geom_vline(xintercept = Y_i / X_i, 
             color = "#EB7B35", linetype = "dashed", size = 0.8) +
  labs(
    #title = "Squared Residuals as a Function of the fixed slope",
    x = expression(beta[1]),
    y = "Squared Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +                     # readable font
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0, face = "bold"), # left-align and bold title
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "black")
  )

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','ols_boxes_plot.png'), plot = p6, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)

# Seattle Children's official colors include:
#Teal ("#00B5AD")
#Bright Green ("#8DC63F")
#Light Blue ("#00A9E0")
#Warm Gray ("#A7A9AC")
#Dark Gray ("#4B4F54")
#orange ("#EB7B35")

```

.center[

<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','ols_boxes_plot.png')` style="width:300px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">

]  

<small>

The squared-error function forms a **smooth quadratic surface.**

This means: `r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` there is **one optimal solution** `r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` we can solve it analytically

.center[
.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
OLS therefore has a closed-form estimator.
</span>**
]
]

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**But What Is OLS Really Doing?**

<small>

> When errors are normally distributed, the OLS estimates of the regression coefficients are identical to the maximum likelihood estimates - minimizing squared residuals and maximizing the normal likelihood are the same problem.

To see why, we need to introduce **Maximum Likelihood (ML)**.

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

<style="clear: both;">

.center[
.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
Maximum Likelihood
</span>**
]
]

A **probability density function `r emo::ji("smirk")`** describes the distribution of the data.

$$ 
\color{grey}{\text{For a normal distribution: }}
\large
\text{L}_i = {\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^2}} \text{e}^{-.5\frac{(\color{#EB7B35}{\text{Y}_i - \mu})^2 }{\color{#EB7B35}{\sigma}^2} }} 
$$

$\text{L}_i$ measures the relative probability of observing $\text{Y}_i$ given a mean and variance.

---

.pull-right-60[

```{r, out.width = "450px", out.height = "425px", echo = F, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, dpi = 300}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(smplot2)

# Data for each row in the table
ID <- 1:20
x <- c(36.6, 41.8, 42.6, 43.1, 43.4, 44.2, 44.9, 46.3, 48.6, 49.0, 
       50.0, 51.6, 54.6, 54.8, 55.7, 57.2, 57.6, 60.3, 60.9, 65.3)

data1 <- data.frame(ID, x)

# Data for each row in the table
ID <- 1:20
x <- c(36.6, 41.8, 42.6, 43.1, 43.4, 44.2, 44.9, 46.3, 48.6, 49.0, 
       50.0, 51.6, 54.6, 54.8, 55.7, 57.2, 57.6, 60.3, 60.9, 65.3)
y <- c(40.0, 40.0, 35.0, 43.0, 42.6, 39.0, 45.0, 45.2, 50.0, 40.0, 
        NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA)

# Create the main data frame
datam <- data.frame(ID, x, y)

#--- fiml 

mu <- mean(data1$x, na.rm=T)
sigma <- sd(data1$x, na.rm=T)

data1$Li <- round(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5), 6) 

```

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Find the largest value in the last column
data1 <- data1 %>%
  mutate(across(
    .cols = ncol(data1),
    .fns = ~ ifelse(. == max(., na.rm = TRUE), 
                    paste0("<b style='font-size:16px;'>", ., "</b>"), 
                    .)
  ))

# Rename the last column to "L_i" with a subscript
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)] <- "<div style='text-align: center;'>L<sub><i>i</i></sub></div>"

# Create the table with kable and apply styling
knitr::kable(
  data1,
  format = "html",
  align = "ll",
  escape = FALSE
) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
    font_size = 13.5, full_width = TRUE
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::row_spec(11, background = "#8DC63F", color = "white")

```

]

.pull-left-40[

**ML estimation**

.small[

Applying the density function gives the relative probability $\text{(}L_i\text{)}$ of each score from this normal distribution. 

**Complete data:** 

$$
\text{x } (\mu = `r round(mu, 2)` \text{, } \sigma = `r round(sigma, 2)`)
$$
]

.center[
.highlight[
**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">
Largest $L_i$ is best!
</span>**
]
]

]

---

```{r, out.width = "600px", out.height = "600px", echo = F, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, dpi = 300}

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)

data1$likelihood <- 1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(-0.5 * ((data1$x - mu)^2) / sigma^2)

df <- data.frame(x = seq(25, 75, by = 0.5))
df$likelihood <- 1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(-0.5 * ((df$x - mu)^2) / sigma^2)

# Define the x positions for vertical lines and find the closest mu values
select_x1 <- 45
select_x2 <- 62


# Create the plot
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = likelihood)) +
  geom_line(color = "#00A9E0", size = 3.50) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(df$likelihood)), limits = c(0, max(df$likelihood)+.01), expand = c(0, 0))+

 # Left 
  annotate("text", x = select_x1 - 7, y = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x1], 
           label = paste0("italic(L)[", sprintf("%.0f", select_x1), "] == ", 
                         sprintf("%.2f", df$likelihood[df$x == select_x1])),
           parse = TRUE, color = "#4B4F54", size = 6) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = select_x1, y = min(likelihood), 
                   xend = select_x1, yend = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x1]), 
               color = "#A7A9AC", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = select_x1, y = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x1]), 
             color = "#8DC63F", size = 6) +
  
  # Right
  annotate("text", x = select_x2 + 7, y = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x2], 
           label = paste0("italic(L)[", sprintf("%.0f", select_x2), "] == ", 
                         sprintf("%.2f", df$likelihood[df$x == select_x2])),
           parse = TRUE, color = "#4B4F54", size = 6) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = select_x2, y = min(likelihood), 
                   xend = select_x2, yend = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x2]), 
               color = "#A7A9AC", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = select_x2, y = df$likelihood[df$x == select_x2]), 
             color = "#8DC63F", size = 6) +

  labs(title = "", x = "Mean (x)", y = "Likelihood") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = unit(c(1, 1, 1, 1), "cm"),
    panel.background = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 22, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 15)),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 22, color = "black", face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

```

---

.pull-right-40[

```{r, out.width = "600px", out.height = "600px", echo = F, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, dpi = 300}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(smplot2)

# Data for each row in the table
ID <- 1:20
x <- c(36.6, 41.8, 42.6, 43.1, 43.4, 44.2, 44.9, 46.3, 48.6, 49.0, 
            50.0, 51.6, 54.6, 54.8, 55.7, 57.2, 57.6, 60.3, 60.9, 65.3)

data1 <- data.frame(ID, x)

# Data for each row in the table
ID <- 1:20
x <- c(36.6, 41.8, 42.6, 43.1, 43.4, 44.2, 44.9, 46.3, 48.6, 49.0, 
            50.0, 51.6, 54.6, 54.8, 55.7, 57.2, 57.6, 60.3, 60.9, 65.3)
y <- c(40.0, 40.0, 35.0, 43.0, 42.6, 39.0, 45.0, 45.2, 50.0, 40.0, 
            NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA)

# Create the main data frame
datam <- data.frame(ID, x, y)

#--- fiml 

mu <- mean(data1$x, na.rm=T)
sigma <- sd(data1$x, na.rm=T)

data1$Li <- round(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5), 6)
data1$logLi <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 6)

options(scipen = 999)
smallval <- prod(data1$Li)
smallval <- as.character(smallval)
options(scipen = 0)

sumfit <- round(sum(data1$logLi),2)

```

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Find the largest value in the last column
data1 <- data1 %>%
  mutate(across(
    .cols = ncol(data1),
    .fns = ~ ifelse(. == max(., na.rm = TRUE), 
                    paste0("<b style='font-size:16px;'>", ., "</b>"), 
                    .)
  ))

# Rename the last column to "L_i" with a subscript
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)-1] <- "<div style='text-align: center;'>L<sub><i>i</i></sub></div>"
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)] <- "<div style='text-align: center;'>logL<sub><i>i</i></sub></div>"


# Create the table with kable and apply styling
knitr::kable(
  data1,
  format = "html",
  align = "ll",
  escape = FALSE
) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
    font_size = 13.5, full_width = TRUE
  )  %>%
  kableExtra::row_spec(11, background = "#8DC63F", color = "white")

```

]

.pull-left-40[

**Maximum Likelihood**

.small[
Multiple each $(L_i)$ to get sample likelihood.

<small><small><small>`r smallval`</small></small></small> 
<small><small>Fit of this data to $\mu$ = `r sprintf("%.2f", mu)`, $\sigma$ = `r sprintf("%.2f", sigma)`</small></small> 

<small>**To avoid small numbers, we take the log of the likelihood.**</small>  

Add each $logL_i$ to get sample loglikelihood.

`r sumfit`

**Model Fit Indices use this!**

$\small \text{AIC} = -2 \times logL+2k$
$\small \text{ BIC} = -2 \times logL+k \times logN$

]

]

---

.pull-left-60[

```{r, out.width = "600px", out.height = "600px", echo = F, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, dpi = 300}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(smplot2)

# Data for each row in the table
ID <- 1:20
x <- c(36.6, 41.8, 42.6, 43.1, 43.4, 44.2, 44.9, 46.3, 48.6, 49.0, 
            50.0, 51.6, 54.6, 54.8, 55.7, 57.2, 57.6, 60.3, 60.9, 65.3)

data1 <- data.frame(ID, x)

#--- fiml 

mu1 <- 30
mu2 <- 40
mu3 <- 50
mu4 <- 60
mu5 <- 70

sigma <- sd(data1$x, na.rm=T)

data1$logLi1 <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu1)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 3)
data1$logLi2 <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu2)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 3)
data1$logLi3 <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu3)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 3)
data1$logLi4 <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu4)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 3)
data1$logLi5 <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu5)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 3)

#data1$logLi <- round(log(1 / sqrt(2 * pi * sigma^2) * exp(((data1$x - mu)^2 / sigma^2) * -0.5)), 6)

sumfit1 <- round(sum(data1$logLi1), 2)
sumfit2 <- round(sum(data1$logLi2), 2)
sumfit3 <- round(sum(data1$logLi3), 2)
sumfit4 <- round(sum(data1$logLi4), 2)
sumfit5 <- round(sum(data1$logLi5), 2)

new_row <- c("", "", "", "", "", "", "")

data1 <- rbind(data1, new_row)

new_row <- c("", "", sumfit1, sumfit2, sumfit3, sumfit4, sumfit5)

data1 <- rbind(data1, new_row)

```

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Rename the last column to "L_i" with a subscript
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)-4] <- paste0("\u03BC = ", mu1)
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)-3] <- paste0("\u03BC = ", mu2)
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)-2] <- paste0("\u03BC = ", mu3)
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)-1] <- paste0("\u03BC = ", mu4)
colnames(data1)[ncol(data1)] <- paste0("\u03BC = ", mu5)

# Bold the entire last row
data1 <- data1 %>%
  mutate(across(
    everything(),  # Apply to all columns
    .fns = ~ ifelse(row_number() == n(), 
                    paste0("<b style='font-size:12px;'>", ., "</b>"), 
                    .)
  ))

# Create the table with kable and apply styling
knitr::kable(
  data1,
  format = "html",
  align = "ll",
  escape = FALSE
) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
    font_size = 11, full_width = TRUE
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::column_spec(5, background = ifelse(seq_len(nrow(data1)) == nrow(data1), "#8DC63F", "transparent"),
                             color = ifelse(seq_len(nrow(data1)) == nrow(data1), "white", "black"))

```

]

.pull-right-40[

<small>

**ML estimation as an audition process**

<small><small>

> We calculate the log-likelihood for every observation under each candidate μ, then sum them up. The total at the bottom of each column is that candidate's total log-likelihood score - **its audition result**.

- Here, the highest log-likelihood (least negative) wins!

</small></small>

</small>

]

---

```{r olsml, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, eval=FALSE}

library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(animation)
library(dplyr)
library(here)

# ── Data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
beta  <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 400)
df1   <- data.frame(beta, S    = (beta - 0.5)^2 + 1)
df2   <- data.frame(beta, logL = -(beta - 0.5)^2 + 3)

# Frames: positions of the travelling tangent point
frames <- seq(-2.5, 2.5, length.out = 80)

# Build a params table — one row per animation frame
params <- data.frame(beta0 = frames) %>%
  mutate(
    S0    = (beta0 - 0.5)^2 + 1,
    L0    = -(beta0 - 0.5)^2 + 3,
    slope_ols = 2  * (beta0 - 0.5),
    slope_ml  = -2 * (beta0 - 0.5)
  )

# ── Frame-drawing function ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plot_frame <- function(b0, S0, L0, slope_ols, slope_ml) {
  
  # Tangent line data for this frame
  tang_ols <- data.frame(
    beta    = beta,
    tangent = S0 + slope_ols * (beta - b0)
  )
  tang_ml <- data.frame(
    beta    = beta,
    tangent = L0 + slope_ml  * (beta - b0)
  )
  
  # ── OLS panel ──
  p1 <- ggplot() +
    geom_line(data = df1,      aes(beta, S),       linewidth = 2, color = "#2C6BAA") +
    geom_line(data = tang_ols, aes(beta, tangent),  linewidth = 1.5,   color = "orange") +
    geom_point(aes(x = b0, y = S0), color = "red", size = 6) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 12)) +
    labs(
      title = "OLS: Searching for slope = 0",
      y     = expression(S(beta)),
      x     = expression(beta)
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 22)
  
  # ── ML panel ──
  p2 <- ggplot() +
    geom_line(data = df2,     aes(beta, logL),    linewidth = 2, color = "#2E9F65") +
    geom_line(data = tang_ml, aes(beta, tangent), linewidth = 1.5,   color = "orange") +
    geom_point(aes(x = b0, y = L0), color = "red", size = 6) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-7, 4)) +
    labs(
      title = "ML: Searching for slope = 0",
      y     = expression(log(L)(beta)),
      x     = expression(beta)
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 22)
  
  # ── Combined ──
  combined <- (p1 | p2) +
    plot_annotation(
      title    = "OLS and ML Find the Point Where the Derivative = 0"#,
     # subtitle = expression(epsilon %~% N(0, sigma^2))
    )
  
  print(combined)
}

# ── Save GIF ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 1280 × 720 = standard 720p, matches PowerPoint 16:9 slide (13.33" × 7.5" at 96 dpi)

output_path <- here("slides","img","ols_ml_derivative.gif")

saveGIF(
  {
    # Animate: tangent sweeps across the curve
    for (i in seq_len(nrow(params))) {
      plot_frame(
        b0        = params$beta0[i],
        S0        = params$S0[i],
        L0        = params$L0[i],
        slope_ols = params$slope_ols[i],
        slope_ml  = params$slope_ml[i]
      )
    }
    
    # Hold on final (optimal) frame for ~2 seconds
    opt <- params[which.min(abs(params$beta0 - 0.5)), ]
    for (j in 1:20) {
      plot_frame(
        b0        = opt$beta0,
        S0        = opt$S0,
        L0        = opt$L0,
        slope_ols = opt$slope_ols,
        slope_ml  = opt$slope_ml
      )
    }
  },
  movie.name = output_path,
  interval   = 0.1,
  ani.width  = 1280,   # 16:9 at 720p — fits a full PowerPoint slide
  ani.height = 720
)

# message("GIF saved to: ", output_path)

```

.center[

`r knitr::include_graphics(here::here("slides", "img", "ols_ml_derivative.gif"))`

]  

<small><small>

> SSR is embedded inside the log-likelihood given errors are normal and independent with constant variance, so both methods locate the same optimal $\beta$.

</small></small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**Why Move Beyond OLS?**
  
OLS works only for:

- Normal outcomes

- One outcome at a time

- Independent observations

- Listwise deletion with missing data

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**Maximum likelihood allows us to estimate:**

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` multilevel models

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` generalized models (binary, count, etc.)

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` models with missing data (FIML)

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` multiple outcomes simultaneously

> ML is the general estimation framework for modern statistical models. We will use it!

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**ML in Multilevel Models**

Multilevel models estimate:

- Fixed effects

- Random effects

- Variance components

These parameters are estimated using **ML** or **REML**.

---

## `r emo::ji("bullseye")` Estimation in Statistical Models

**ML vs. Restricted/Residual ML (REML)**

<small><small>
  
| Method | Use Case | Pros / Notes |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| **ML** | Comparing models with **different fixed effects** | Random effects estimates may be slightly biased downward |
| **REML** | Comparing models with the **same fixed effects**, or interpreting random effects | Provides unbiased estimates of random effects var/covar<br><br> Cannot compare models with different fixed effects |

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` Changing fixed effects? - Use ML to compare

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` Changing random effects only? - Use REML to compare

`r emo::ji("white_check_mark")` Final model for reporting? - Refit with REML for unbiased variance estimates.

</small></small>

---

## How to handle nested data?

---

## How to handle nested data?

Methods fall into three general categories based on how they handle nestedness.

--

- `r ji('warning')` **Ignore:** disaggregation, aggregation   

- `r ji('confused')` **Control:** ANCOVA, separate regressions, adjusting the results, fixed effects approach 

- `r ji('mag')` **Model:** *Modeling* nestedness     

--

> In fact, methods have shifted from *controlling clustering* (attempting to remove its influence) to **modeling it as something worth studying**! `r ji('raised_hands')`

---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

> Disaggregation means .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">moving variables to a lower level</span>**] as if there is no clustering <small>(see [Hox, J., Moerbeek, M., & Van de Schoot, R., 2017](https://biostats.bepress.com/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1001&context=bioconductor)).</small>

- **The risk:** Inflated sample size and underestimated standard errors (false positives).


---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

```{r disaggregation, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)  # wide sd = lots of overlap
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))

group_colors <- c("#E41A1C","#377EB8","#4DAF4A","#FF7F00","#984EA3")

# group means
group_means <- aggregate(cbind(x, y) ~ group, data = df, FUN = mean)

label_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  sub <- df[df$group == g, ]
  data.frame(
    group = factor(g),
    x     = max(sub$x) + 1,
    y     = mean(sub$y),
    label = paste0("Group ", g),
    color = group_colors[g]
  )
}))


# HTML formula using ggtext richtext
formula_label <- "<span style='font-size:18pt'><i>y<sub>i</sub></i> = <i>β<sub>0</sub></i> + <i>β<sub>1</sub></i><i>x<sub>i</sub></i> + <i>e<sub>i</sub></i></span>"


p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group, group = group)) +
  
  # raw points, faded
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.15) +
  
  # within-group regression lines
  #geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,
  #            linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
  
  # pooled OLS through all raw points
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "#222222", linewidth = 1.8) +
  
  # group mean points
  #geom_point(data = group_means,
  #           aes(x = x, y = y, color = group),
  #           size = 8, alpha = 1, shape = 18) +
  
  # regression line through group means (aggregated)
  #geom_smooth(data = group_means,
  #            aes(x = x, y = y, group = 1),
  #            method = "lm", se = FALSE,
  #            color = "#2166AC", linewidth = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  
  #geom_text(
  #  data     = label_df,
  #  aes(x = x, y = y, label = label, color = group),
  #  hjust    = 0,
  #  fontface = "bold",
  #  size     = 6,
  #  show.legend = FALSE
  #) +
  
  # richtext annotation for the formula
  annotate(
    "richtext",
    x         = min(df$x),
    y         = max(df$y),
    label     = formula_label,
    hjust     = -0.10,
    vjust     = 1,
    fill      = NA,       # no box background
    label.color = NA      # no box border
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = group_colors) +

  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df$x)),
      ceiling(max(df$x)+5),
      by = 10                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.10))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df$y)),
      ceiling(max(df$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.20))
  )+
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")

#p

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','disaggregation_plot.png'), plot = p, width = 6.5, height = 4, dpi = 300)

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','disaggregation_plot.png')` style="width:600px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

<small>

- Level-1 units are the unit of analysis.
- Level-1 and level-2 predictors are included as if they were ordinary independent variables.

</small>

--- 

## `r emo::ji("flushed")` Not recommended

<small>

> .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">Violates the independence assumption</span>**] which can lead to standard errors that are severely biased (usually underestimated).   

- **Result:** Inflated Type I error rates and overconfident conclusions.

> .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">Neglects between-cluster variation</span>**], effectively treating all clusters as identical and failing to model the unique intercepts or slopes associated with each group.

- **Result:** Lost insights into group-level effects and potentially misleading fixed effects estimates.

</small>


---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

.center[

.pull-left-40[

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)  # wide sd = lots of overlap
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
})) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 1)))

df %>%
  arrange(group) %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  kable() %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 14)

```

]

.pull-right-60[

.left[

<small>

**Example (Nested/Grouped Data)**

- **x** = predictor, **y** = outcome

- **5 groups**, showing first 3 rows 

- 80 individuals per group (*N* = 400)

- **Think:** students in classrooms, patients in hospitals, repeated measures per person

$$\text{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{x}_i + \text{e}_i$$

</small>

] ]

]


---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

<small><small>

**R code:**

<pre style="font-size:0.8em;">
pooled_lm <- lm(y ~ x, data = df)
summary(pooled_lm)
</pre>

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(glue)

set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)  # wide sd = lots of overlap
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))

# ---------------------------
# 1. Center predictor
# ---------------------------
# grand-mean
df <- df %>%
  mutate(x_c = x - mean(x))

# group-mean
#df <- df %>%
#  group_by(group) %>%
#  mutate(x_gc = x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
#  ungroup()

# ---------------------------
# 2. Pooled OLS with centered predictor
# ---------------------------
pooled_lm <- lm(y ~ x_c, data = df)

# ---------------------------
# 3. Standardized beta (manual)
# ---------------------------
x_sd <- sd(df$x_c)
y_sd <- sd(df$y)
beta_std <- round(coef(pooled_lm)["x_c"] * (x_sd / y_sd), 3)

# ---------------------------
# 4. Effect size f²
# ---------------------------
r2 <- summary(pooled_lm)$r.squared
f2 <- r2 / (1 - r2)

# Convert f2 to r for interpretation
r_eff <- r2 #sqrt(f2 / (1 + f2))

# R2 Cohen
effect_desc <- case_when(
  r_eff < 0.02              ~ "negligible",
  r_eff >= 0.02 & r_eff < 0.13 ~ "small",
  r_eff >= 0.13 & r_eff < 0.26 ~ "medium",
  r_eff >= 0.26             ~ "large"
)

# ---------------------------
# 5. Tidy table for slide
# ---------------------------
pooled_table <- tidy(pooled_lm) %>%
  dplyr::select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 2),
    p.value = round(p.value, 3)
  )

kable(pooled_table, caption = "Pooled OLS Regression with Centered Predictor")

# ---------------------------
# 6. Automated APA-style interpretation
# ---------------------------
intercept <- pooled_table$estimate[pooled_table$term == "(Intercept)"]
slope     <- pooled_table$estimate[pooled_table$term == "x_c"]
t_slope   <- pooled_table$statistic[pooled_table$term == "x_c"]
p_slope   <- pooled_table$p.value[pooled_table$term == "x_c"]

#In a linear regression predicting Y from X (mean-centered), the intercept was **`r intercept`**, representing the expected *Y* at the mean of *X*. The slope for *X* was **`r slope`**, which was `r {ifelse(p_slope < 0.05, 'statistically significant', 'not statistically significant')}`, *t*(`r pooled_lm$df.residual`) = `r t_slope`, *p* = `r p_slope`, with a standardized beta of `r beta_std` and an effect size $R^2$ = `r round(r2,3)`, indicating a `r effect_desc` proportion of variance in *Y* explained by *X*. .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">This analysis assumes that groups have no influence; the more group membership actually affects Y, the more misleading this result becomes</span>**].

```

> In a linear regression predicting Y from X (mean-centered), the intercept was **`r intercept`**, representing the expected *Y* at the mean of *X*. The slope for *X* was **`r slope`**, which was `r {ifelse(p_slope < 0.05, 'statistically significant', 'not statistically significant')}`, *t*(`r pooled_lm$df.residual`) = `r t_slope`, *p* = `r p_slope`. .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">This analysis assumes that groups have no influence; the more group membership actually affects Y, the more misleading this result becomes</span>**].

</small></small>

---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
set.seed(1234)
n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80
group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)
df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))
group_colors <- c("#E41A1C","#377EB8","#4DAF4A","#FF7F00","#984EA3")
group_means <- aggregate(cbind(x, y) ~ group, data = df, FUN = mean)
label_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  sub <- df[df$group == g, ]
  data.frame(
    group = factor(g),
    x     = max(sub$x) + 1,
    y     = mean(sub$y),
    label = paste0("Group ", g),
    color = group_colors[g]
  )
}))

g1 <- df[df$group == 1, ]
g5 <- df[df$group == 5, ]

formula_label <- "<span style='font-size:18pt'><i>y<sub>i</sub></i> = 6.391 + 0.001<i>x<sub>i</sub></i> + <i>e<sub>i</sub></i></span>"

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group, group = group)) +
  
  # green circle around each Group 1 point
  geom_point(
    data        = g1,
    aes(x = x, y = y),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    shape       = 21,
    size        = 5,
    color       = "green3",
    fill        = NA,
    stroke      = 1.2
  ) +
  
  # green circle around each Group 1 point
  geom_point(
    data        = g5,
    aes(x = x, y = y),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    shape       = 21,
    size        = 5,
    color       = "red",
    fill        = NA,
    stroke      = 1.2
  ) +  
  
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.10) +
  
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "#222222", linewidth = 1.8) +
  
  annotate(
    "richtext",
    x         = min(df$x),
    y         = max(df$y),
    label     = formula_label,
    hjust     = -0.10,
    vjust     = 1,
    fill      = NA,
    label.color = NA
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = group_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(floor(min(df$x)), ceiling(max(df$x) + 5), by = 10),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.10))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(floor(min(df$y)), ceiling(max(df$y) + 5), by = 5),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.20))
  ) +
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")

#p

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','grouped_residual_plot.png'), plot = p, width = 6.5, height = 4, dpi = 300)

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','grouped_residual_plot.png')` style="width:600px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

<small>

**Knowing the group matters!**

- `r emo::ji("warning")` $\text{e}_i$ = individual + group variation
 

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("see_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("hear_no_evil")` `r emo::ji("speak_no_evil")` What is disaggregation?

.center[

.pull-left-40[

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(htmltools)   # <-- needed for HTML()

set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)  # wide sd = lots of overlap
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
})) 

# ---------------------------
# 1. Center predictor
# ---------------------------
# grand-mean
df <- df %>%
  mutate(x_c = x - mean(x))

x_var_total <- sum((df$x_c)^2)

# ---------------------------
# 2. Pooled OLS with centered predictor
# ---------------------------
pooled_lm <- lm(y ~ x_c, data = df)

# Add predicted values and residuals
#df$y_hat <- predict(pooled_lm)
df$resid <- resid(pooled_lm)

sigma2_pooled <- var(df$resid)  # residual variance
se_naive      <- sqrt(sigma2_pooled / x_var_total)

library(lme4)
library(performance)  # for icc()

# 1. Random intercept model (empty predictor)
mlm <- lmer(y ~ 1 + (1 | group), data = df)

# Get ICC in one line
icc <- as.numeric(performance::icc(mlm)[1])

# Adjust for intra-group correlation
rho <- icc         # estimated ICC
n_j <- n_per_group # points per group
se_corrected <- sqrt(sigma2_pooled * (1 + (n_j - 1) * rho) / x_var_total)

df <- df %>%
  dplyr::select(-x_c) %>%
  # Round all numeric columns to 1 decimal place
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 1)))

# Show first 3 cases per group
df %>%
  arrange(group) %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    resid = cell_spec(
      resid,
      format = "html",                                          
      background = ifelse(resid < 0, "#EB7B35", "#8DC63F"),
      color = "white"
    )
  ) %>%
  kable(format = "html", escape = FALSE) %>%                   
  kable_styling(font_size = 14)

# Seattle Children's official colors include:
#Teal ("#00B5AD")
#Bright Green ("#8DC63F")
#Light Blue ("#00A9E0")
#Warm Gray ("#A7A9AC")
#Dark Gray ("#4B4F54")
#orange ("#EB7B35")

```

]

.pull-left-60[

.left[

<small>

**Where the bias shows up:**

<small>

The model **<span style="color:#8DC63F;">overpredicts</span>** some groups and **<span style="color:#EB7B35;">underpredicts</span>** others.

> Residual variance is inflated but the **SE of the slope is too low** because OLS treats all `r n_per_group*n_groups` points as independent. Accounting for strong within-group correlation shrinks the effective sample size from `r n_per_group*n_groups` to `r round((n_per_group*n_groups/(1+((n_per_group-1)*icc))),0)`! `r emo::ji("astonished")` 

$$
\small
\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2 \times \color{#A7A9AC}{\text{VIF}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}} \text{, where} \color{#A7A9AC}{\text{VIF}} = 1 + (n_j - 1)\rho 
$$
Our estimated SE was **`r round(se_naive,3)`**, but adjusting for groups it should be **`r round(se_corrected,2)`**. 

</small></small>

] ]

]

---

## `r emo::ji("link")` What is aggregation?

---

## `r emo::ji("link")` What is aggregation?

```{r aggregation, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80

group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)  # wide sd = lots of overlap
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))

group_colors <- c("#E41A1C","#377EB8","#4DAF4A","#FF7F00","#984EA3")

# group means
group_means <- aggregate(cbind(x, y) ~ group, data = df, FUN = mean)

label_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  sub <- df[df$group == g, ]
  data.frame(
    group = factor(g),
    x     = mean(sub$x) + 2.5,
    y     = mean(sub$y)+ 1.25,
    label = paste0("Group ", g),
    color = group_colors[g]
  )
}))


label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 3"] <- 
  label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 3"] + 0.50

label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 1"] <- 
  label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 1"] + 1

label_df$x[label_df$label == "Group 1"] <- 
  label_df$x[label_df$label == "Group 1"] - 1

# HTML formula using ggtext richtext
formula_label <- "<span style='font-size:18pt'><i>y<sub>j</sub></i> = <i>β<sub>0</sub></i> + <i>β<sub>1</sub></i><i>x<sub>j</sub></i> + <i>e<sub>j</sub></i></span>"


p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, color = group, group = group)) +
  
  # regression line through group means (aggregated)
  geom_smooth(data = group_means,
              aes(x = x, y = y, group = 1),
              method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 2, linetype = "solid") +
  
  # raw points, faded
  geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.03) +
  
  # within-group regression lines
  #geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,
  #            linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
  
  # pooled OLS through all raw points
  #geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
  #            color = "#222222", linewidth = 1.8) +
  
  # group mean points
  geom_point(data = group_means,
             aes(x = x, y = y, color = group),
             size = 8, alpha = 1, shape = 19) +
  
  geom_text(
    data     = label_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label, color = group),
    hjust    = 0,
    fontface = "bold",
    size     = 6,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  
  # richtext annotation for the formula
  annotate(
    "richtext",
    x         = min(df$x),
    y         = max(df$y),
    label     = formula_label,
    hjust     = -0.25,
    vjust     = 1,
    fill      = NA,       # no box background
    label.color = NA      # no box border
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = group_colors) +

  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df$x)),
      ceiling(max(df$x)+5),
      by = 10                                 # more ticks on y
    ),   # more ticks on x
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.20))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(
      floor(min(df$y)),
      ceiling(max(df$y)+5),
      by = 5                                 # more ticks on y
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.20))
  )+
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    axis.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text       = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks      = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  
  labs(x = "Predictor (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)")

#p

ggsave(here::here('slides','img','aggregation_plot.png'), plot = p, width = 6.5, height = 4, dpi = 300)

```

.center[
<img src=`r here::here('slides','img','aggregation_plot.png')` style="width:600px; display:block; margin:-40px auto -20px auto;">
]  

<small>

- Level-2 units are the unit of analysis.
- **Massive loss of information!** `r emo::ji("astonished")`    
- The results cannot be generalized to level-1 (*ecological fallacy*).

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("link")` What is aggregation?

<small><small>

**R code:**

<pre style="font-size:0.8em;">
agg_lm <- lm(y_mean ~ x_mean, data = group_means)
</pre>

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(glue)

# ---------------------------
# 1. Center predictor
# ---------------------------
df <- df %>%
  mutate(x_c = x - mean(x))

# ---------------------------
# 5. Aggregated regression (regression through group means)
# ---------------------------
group_means <- df %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  summarise(x_mean = mean(x_c), y_mean = mean(y), .groups = "drop")

agg_lm <- lm(y_mean ~ x_mean, data = group_means)

# ---------------------------
# 5. Tidy table for slide
# ---------------------------
agg_table <- tidy(agg_lm) %>%
  dplyr::select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 2),
    p.value = round(p.value, 3)
  )

kable(agg_table, caption = "Aggregated Regression (Group Means)")

# ---------------------------
# 6. Automated APA-style interpretation
# ---------------------------
intercept <- agg_table$estimate[agg_table$term == "(Intercept)"]
slope     <- agg_table$estimate[agg_table$term == "x_mean"]
t_slope   <- agg_table$statistic[agg_table$term == "x_mean"]
p_slope   <- agg_table$p.value[agg_table$term == "x_mean"]

# ---------------------------
# 5. Standardized beta and effect size
# ---------------------------
x_sd <- sd(group_means$x_mean)
y_sd <- sd(group_means$y_mean)
beta_std <- round(slope * (x_sd / y_sd), 3)

# f² effect size
r2 <- summary(agg_lm)$r.squared
f2 <- r2 / (1 - r2)

# Convert f² -> r and dynamic description
r_eff <- r2 #sqrt(f2 / (1 + f2))
effect_desc <- case_when(
  r_eff < 0.02              ~ "negligible",
  r_eff >= 0.02 & r_eff < 0.13 ~ "small",
  r_eff >= 0.13 & r_eff < 0.26 ~ "medium",
  r_eff >= 0.26             ~ "large"
)

# ---------------------------
# 6. Automated APA-style interpretation
# ---------------------------

```

> In a linear regression predicting **group-mean Y** from the **group-mean X** (both grand mean-centered). The slope for *X* was **`r round(slope,1)`**, which was `r {ifelse(p_slope < 0.05, 'statistically significant', 'not statistically significant')}`, *t*(`r agg_lm$df.residual`) = `r t_slope`, *p* = `r p_slope`, with a `r effect_desc` effect size of $\text{R}^2$ = `r round(r2,2)`. .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">This analysis is at the group level, and cannot be assumed to apply to individuals within those groups</span>**].

</small></small>

---

## `r emo::ji("hammer_and_wrench")` The fixed effects approach?

---

## `r emo::ji("hammer_and_wrench")` The fixed effects approach?

**Include group as a fixed effect via dummy coding.**

<small>

- Separate intercept for each group

- Slopes equal (unless interactions added)

- Many parameters (50 groups = 49 dummy codes) `r emo::ji("worried")`

<small>

> **Imagine 5 specific hospitals** (Seattle Children's, CHOP, and so on) and we chose these hospitals deliberately because we want to study *these* hospitals specifically. Our estimates tell us how much each hospital's intercept differs from **the reference hospital**, and .highlight[**<span style="color:#FFFFFF;">we never intend to say anything beyond these 5 hospitals anyway</span>**]. Because slopes are fixed (parallel lines), the effect of *X* represents the common within-hospital relationship across all hospitals.

</small></small> 

---

## `r emo::ji("hammer_and_wrench")` The fixed effects approach?

<small><small>

**R code:**

<pre style="font-size:0.8em;">
fixed_lm <- lm(y ~ x + factor(group), data = df)
</pre>

```{r fixedeff, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)

set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80
group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))
group_colors <- c("#E41A1C","#377EB8","#4DAF4A","#FF7F00","#984EA3")

# --- fixed effect: fit model with common slope --------
fixed_lm <- lm(y ~ x + factor(group), data = df)
#summary(fixed_fit)

# ---------------------------
# 5. Tidy table for slide
# ---------------------------
fixed_table <- tidy(fixed_lm) %>%
  dplyr::select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 1),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 1),
    p.value = round(p.value, 3)
  )

kable(fixed_table, caption = "Fixed-effects Regression (dummy codes)")


```

This approach absorbs between-group variation and focuses on within-group associations, producing more realistic SEs than naive pooled OLS.

</small></small>

---

## `r emo::ji("hammer_and_wrench")` The fixed effects approach?

**`r emo::ji("one")` No bias from omitted group effects**

<small>

- The over/under-prediction problem from pooled OLS goes away because each group gets its own intercept.

</small>

**`r emo::ji("two")` No design-effect inflation from clustering**

<small>

- The ICC is controlled by the group terms 

</small>

**`r emo::ji("three")` Effective sample size is `r n_per_group*n_groups - (n_groups-1)` after adjustment.** 

<small>

.center[

*Assumes fixed slopes unless you add interactions.* 

]

</small>

---

## `r emo::ji("bar_chart")` ANCOVA approach?

---

## `r emo::ji("bar_chart")` ANCOVA approach?

```{r ancova, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, eval=T}

library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)

set.seed(1234)

n_groups    <- 5
n_per_group <- 80
group_x_means    <- c(15, 30, 45, 60, 75)
group_intercepts <- c(8, 5, 2, -1, -4)
group_slopes     <- c(0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10)

df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  x_g <- rnorm(n_per_group, mean = group_x_means[g], sd = 20)
  y_g <- group_intercepts[g] + group_slopes[g] * x_g + rnorm(n_per_group, 0, 2)
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_g, y = y_g)
}))
group_colors <- c("#E41A1C","#377EB8","#4DAF4A","#FF7F00","#984EA3")

# group means
group_means <- aggregate(cbind(x, y) ~ group, data = df, FUN = mean)

# ── ANCOVA: fit model with common slope ──────────────────────────────────────
ancova_fit <- lm(y ~ x + group, data = df)   # parallel slopes model
common_slope <- coef(ancova_fit)["x"]

# Build predicted line segments for each group over its x-range
line_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  sub <- df[df$group == g, ]
  x_seq <- seq(min(sub$x), max(sub$x), length.out = 100)
  # predict with group factor set to g
  nd <- data.frame(x = x_seq, group = factor(g, levels = 1:n_groups))
  data.frame(group = factor(g), x = x_seq, y = predict(ancova_fit, newdata = nd))
}))

label_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_groups, function(g) {
  sub <- df[df$group == g, ]
  data.frame(
    group = factor(g),
    x     = max(sub$x) + 1.5,
    y     = max(sub$y) - 3,
    label = paste0("Group ", g),
    color = group_colors[g]
  )
}))
label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 4"] <- label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 4"] + 1
label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 2"] <- label_df$y[label_df$label == "Group 2"] + 0.50
```
